retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia With Variant Genetics -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The lymph node architecture is effaced by small mature lymphocytes with condensed nuclear chromatin. Pseudofollicles have a pale nodular appearance due to the proliferation of large lymphocytes having open nuclear chromatin and more abundant cytoplasm.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Epigenetic Mechanism -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Briefly, negatively charged DNA is packaged around a positively charged histone protein octamer, which contains 2 copies of histone proteins H2A, H2B, H3, and H4. [2] This nucleoprotein complex is a nucleosome, the basic unit of chromatin. [3] The nucleosomes of a continuous DNA polymer are connected by linker DNA and the complex is stabilized by histone protein H1. The aggregation of chromatin results in the formation of a chromosome. The chromatin of a chromosome exists as either loose, transcriptionally active euchromatin or dense, transcriptionally inactive heterochromatin. [4] Chemical alterations to histone proteins can induce the formation of either the open euchromatin state, which facilitates gene expression by allowing transcription factors and enzymes to interact with the DNA, or the closed heterochromatin state, which suppresses gene expression by preventing initiation of transcription.

retrieved_docs.title: Biphenotypic Sinonasal Sarcoma -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The histopathologic examination of BSNS demonstrates hypercellular, uniform, long spindle-shaped cells in a fascicular pattern that is occasionally described as a "herringbone" configuration. [6] Nuclei appear tapered; despite the hypercellularity, there are almost no mitoses. Necrosis, ulceration, and hemorrhage are not common. [8] [9] [7] The cytoplasm of involved cells is scant and pale, and the tumor appears unencapsulated. [11] Foci of respiratory epithelium can be entrapped in tumor cells, creating a pseudoglandular appearance. [8] [9] Infiltrative tumors are embedded in a bony matrix. [8] Vascular staghorn formations are common but can confound the diagnosis by expanding the differential diagnosis. [8] [9] [7] [15] Identifying elements of both neural and myogenic differentiation with immunophenotyping, patchy or diffuse S-100 smooth muscle actin expression, and SOX-10 negativity are key in confirming the diagnosis. [6] [8] [9] Genetic features displayed by BSNS involve chromosomal translation t(2,4) and a fusion protein formed by the interaction of the PAX3-MAML genes. [6] [8] The PAX3-MAML fusion is highly reliable in diagnosing BSNS, as it is present in 79 to 96% of cases. [8] The fusion of PAX3 with other genes, NCOA1 and FOXO1, suggests a more aggressive rhabdomyoblastic variant due to different amplification targets downstream. [8] [9] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Glandular Cells (AGS) -- Anatomical Pathology -- Criteria:
retrieved_docs.content: Cells occurring in sheets and strips, with crowding, overlapping nuclei, and pseudo-stratification Enlarged nuclei, up to about 3- 5 times the normal endocervical cells Minor variation in nuclear size and shape Mild nuclear hyperchromasia Mild irregularity of chromatin Rare nucleoli Rare mitotic figures Abundant cytoplasm with a fairly increased nuclear-cytoplasmic ratio Distinct cell borders

retrieved_docs.title: Job Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Job Syndrome (Hyper-IgE syndrome) is a rare, primary immunodeficiency distinguished by the clinical triad of atopic dermatitis, recurrent skin staphylococcal infections, and recurrent pulmonary infections. The disease is characterized by elevated IgE levels with an early onset in primary childhood. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of Job syndrome and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in educating patients about their prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Anti-NMDAR Encephalitis in Children -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Iizuka et al. postulated that NMDAR is present in high density in the frontotemporal area, which atrophies in anti-NMDAR encephalitis leading to changes in behavior, learning, and memory. [23] Research utilizing a super-resolution microscopy model and Monte Carlo simulation found the importance of the interaction between NMDAR with other proteins (e.g., with EphB2R [ephrin-type B receptor 2] and other unknown interacting proteins). [24] Disruption of these interactions reproduced the observed clinical symptoms seen in patients. Drugs such as phencyclidine can mimic the symptoms of anti-NMDAR encephalitis as they are an antagonist at the receptor.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, DNA Packaging -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The ability for DNA packaging to be modified at various stages of the cell cycle is important in both DNA replication and cell division as well as transcription. Replication occurs at many origins of replication throughout the DNA strand to accelerate the replication of the entire genome, with each origin separated by approximately 100,000 base pairs. [10] The DNA does not interact with histones during this process to allow for the propagation of the polymerase enzymes. However, when the process is complete, the DNA must reintegrate with the histones to reform nucleosomes and eventually the supercoiled chromosome structure during mitosis. Following cell division, the DNA must again separate from the histone proteins to undergo transcription. This capability for the DNA-histone interactions to be modulated is crucial for the proper growth and function of a cell with malfunctions contributing to disease like hypermethylation in cancer. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Glandular Cells (AGS) -- Anatomical Pathology -- Criteria:
retrieved_docs.content: Cells occurring in sheets and strips, with nuclear crowding, overlap, and/or pseudo-stratification Cell groups resembling rosettes/ or forming glands or feathering Enlarged and elongated nuclei with hyperchromasia Coarse chromatin with heterogeneity Occasional mitoses and/ or apoptotic bodies Increased nuclear-cytoplasmic ratio Ill-defined cell borders

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Scientists have studied specific examples of pseudogenes for their observed roles regarding the regulation of biochemical processes. An example of pseudogene regulation is with Oct4, a transcription factor, and its associated pseudogene. The Oct4 pseudogene’s RNA transcript has been observed to inhibit differentiation with the original Oct4’s RNA transcript. Additionally, researchers found a knockdown or Oct4 pseudogene antisense RNA to increase concentrations of Oct4 and its associated pseudogene. [1] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The KDM6A mutation affects normal growth and development through similar mechanisms of KMT2D , as both genes belong to the same multiprotein complex. KDM6A (also known as UTX ) is the second most pathologic gene, found in less than 5% of patients with Kabuki syndrome. [1] This mutation is inherited in an X-linked manner; the gene is located on Xp11.23 and encodes a histone (H3)-lysine 27-specific demethylase that interacts with KMT2D in regulating gene expression in the activating signal cointegrator-2-containing complex (ASCOM). [1] [3] [5] Mutations of KDM6A include nonsense, frameshift, splice site, missense, large deletions, small insertions, and small deletions; a rare mosaic case of KDM6A has previously been identified. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Chromosomes -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Chromosomes can exist in the previously described tightly packed structure, referred to as heterochromatin, in which methylation of the DNA and other intermolecular forces keep the structure coiled and condensed. For transcription to occur with RNA polymerase and the other required proteins, the tight heterochromatin structure must unwrap to allow the transcription machinery to access the DNA strands. This looser structure with accessible DNA is referred to as euchromatin. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, G Protein Coupled Receptors -- Fundamentals -- Signaling by GPCRs
retrieved_docs.content: The regulatory subunits of PKA (also known as A-kinase) are important for the sub-cellular localization of PKA, which is facilitated by the interaction of A-kinase anchoring proteins (AKAPs) with the regulatory subunits. One of the well-known targets of PKA is CRE-binding protein (CREB); PKA phosphorylates CREB on a specific serine residue. Phosphorylation of CREB allows the recruitment of a transcriptional co-activator CREB-binding protein (CBP), which stimulates the transcription of various target genes. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The KMT2D mutation affects normal growth and development by interrupting histone methylation related to gene expression. This mutated gene was the first pathogenic gene recognized in Kabuki syndrome, also known as MLL2 . Recognized in 2010, this is the most common gene mutation in patients with Kabuki syndrome, found in 75% of cases. [1] [4] This condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern. [3] The proportion of cases caused by a de novo KMT2D mutation is unknown but is likely high. [5] The KMT2D gene is located on chromosome 12q13, which is expressed in most cells in the body. This encodes a large nucleoprotein that regulates histone methyltransferase activity, which methylates the Lys4 residue of histone H3 (H3K4). H3K4Me (methylated H3K4) activates transcriptional initiation sites on up to 75% of human genes. [1] More than 50 mutations at different sites of KMT2D have been found, including nonsense, missense, frameshift mutations, small deletions, and splice site variants. [1] [3] Rare mosaic KMT2D cases have been identified. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, DNA Packaging -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The tight structure of chromatin brings about the problem of accessibility to the DNA by enzymes involved in DNA replication and transcription. Chromatin exists in one of two states: heterochromatin, which is condensed and allows little access by transcription enzymes, and euchromatin, which is loose to allow for interaction with transcription enzymes. The transition between these two states is determined by interactions between the DNA and histone proteins via post-translational modifications to the histone proteins like methylation and acetylation. [6] Methylation generally increases interactions between the DNA and histone, thus suppressing gene expression, whereas acetylation will loosen interactions resulting in greater access by transcription enzymes resulting in increased gene expression. [7] The post-translational modifications to histone proteins underlie the mechanisms of epigenetics, which are defined as alterations to gene expression without changes to the DNA sequence. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Supernumerary Digit -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: A supernumerary digit or polydactyly is a frequently occurring congenital malformation, predominantly affecting the upper limbs. Individuals affected by this condition typically exhibit excessive fingers or toes, exceeding the usual count of 5. Polydactyly can be categorized into 3 distinct classifications—preaxial, postaxial, and central or mesoaxial—each based on the specific anatomical location involved. Although supernumerary digits typically manifest as isolated occurrences, they can also be associated with specific syndromes. This activity reviews the etiology, pathophysiology, evaluation, and surgical interventions related to supernumerary digits. This topic also highlights the significance of the interprofessional healthcare team in identifying syndromic associations or potential involvement of other organs by conducting comprehensive evaluations to assess for underlying conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Chromosomes -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A chromosome is a DNA molecule that contains the genetic information for an organism. The chromosomal structure is composed of the organism's DNA and special proteins to form the dense, coiled architecture. The chromosome's tertiary structure is a crucial component in transcription regulation and cellular replication, and division.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2554
retrieved_docs.content: Bard-Pic syndrome and primary malignant neoplasm of head of pancreas are both mapped to cancer of pancreas, indicating that Bard-Pic syndrome is related to pancreatic cancer and that the primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas represents a specific form of pancreatic cancer. This connection highlights Bard-Pic syndrome as a risk factor for pancreatic malignancy and underscores the clinical relevance of identifying tumors located in the pancreatic head.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 257
retrieved_docs.content: importance of assessing metastatic potential even when the primary lesion’s benign versus malignant nature is unclear. Collectively, these interrelated entities form a hierarchical and functional network that connects genetic syndromes, primary gastrointestinal cancers, various metastatic malignancies, and skin melanomas, providing essential context for accurate diagnosis, staging, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2918
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells represent the abnormal cellular component that defines and drives the disease known as السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer), indicating that the presence of these atypical, cancerous cells is the hallmark of this specific malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2902
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are abnormal cells that characterize cancers classified as other and unspecified primary, indicating that these atypical cells constitute the pathological basis of such unspecified primary malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 246
retrieved_docs.content: these relationships integrate organ‑specific primary malignancies, metastatic pathways to lymph nodes and bone, and broader cancer classification schemes, providing a structured framework essential for accurate diagnosis, coding, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1899
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is linked to pancreatic cancer, indicating that this genetic or clinical syndrome has a mapped association with the development of cancer of the pancreas. Recognizing this connection is important for clinicians, as patients diagnosed with Bard‑Pic syndrome may require heightened surveillance for pancreatic malignancy and tailored management strategies to address the potential increased risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is a finding of disease and is categorized as cancer; other and unspecified primary, indicating that the presence of malignant cellular infiltrate reflects an underlying malignant neoplasm of unknown primary origin. Recognizing this finding is essential for guiding further diagnostic work‑up and management of patients with cancers where the primary site cannot be identified.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2581
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, malignant and tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant are both classified as abnormal cells of disease and are linked to cancer; other and unspecified primary, with a specific association to pancreatic cancer (السرطان البنكرياسي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 240
retrieved_docs.content: similarly defined as a primary malignant neoplastic proliferation. In addition, an inverse‑is‑a relationship links the entity “سرطانة توتية” to the primary malignant tumor morphology (“ورم خبيث أولي”), further emphasizing its classification as a type of primary malignant neoplasm. Together, these relationships illustrate how various primary malignant neoplasms across different organs are unified by common pathological processes, shared morphological descriptors, and hierarchical links to broader cancer concepts, aiding in consistent classification and clinical understanding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: carcinomas under the broader category of primary malignant neoplasms. Collectively, these relationships delineate a structured view of digestive system cancers, especially pancreatic malignancies, their metastatic characteristics, and their placement within a comprehensive neoplasm classification system that spans multiple organ sites and includes both well‑defined and uncertain neoplastic entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 1129
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate and Metastatic Lesion are both recognized as findings that can represent a Metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular lymph nodes, and this neoplastic involvement is classified as a Secondary Lesion. In addition to indicating metastatic disease in the supraclavicular region, both Malignant Cellular Infiltrate and Metastatic Lesion are documented as findings of a malignant neoplasm without specification of site and are also associated with the Arabic term سرطانة العظم, denoting bone cancer. The broader category of malignant neoplasm without specification of site is linked to clinical documentation elements that describe comorbid conditions relevant to trauma care and the primary diagnostic impression, reflecting its role in comprehensive patient assessment and reporting.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 210
retrieved_docs.content: by multiple Arabic terms (الورم الأورمي الكلوي, سرطان الخلايا الكلوية) and are treated surgically by partial or complete nephrectomy, inversely related to the Arabic procedure term استئصال الكلية; the co‑occurrence of nephrectomy with these renal cancer terms highlights the therapeutic link. Overall, the network provides clinicians with a structured framework for recognizing secondary lesions, interpreting malignant infiltrates, categorizing metastatic spread by organ and histology, and differentiating benign from malignant cellular findings to guide patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1896
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate, Metastatic Lesion, and Secondary Lesion are all recognized as disease‑related findings that point to a Metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular lymph nodes. This metastatic neoplasm to the supraclavicular nodes is itself classified as a Secondary Lesion and is identified by the presence of Malignant Cellular Infiltrate and Metastatic Lesion, while benign tumor cells (Tumor cells, benign) are explicitly noted as not being present. In the context of سرطانة العظم (bone cancer), the same three entities—Malignant Cellular Infiltrate, Metastatic Lesion, and Secondary Lesion—are each considered positive disease findings, reinforcing their role as indicators of metastatic bone cancer involvement. Conversely, benign tumor cells are consistently reported as absent in both the supraclavicular metastatic disease and in سرطانة العظم, underscoring that benign cellular components are not expected in these malignant processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2894
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding that indicates the presence of سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). The detection of malignant cellular infiltrate in tissue samples signals that cancerous cells have invaded the bone, serving as an important diagnostic marker for confirming bone malignancy and guiding subsequent therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2803
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are the abnormal cellular component that defines ovarian cancer, indicating that ovarian cancer is characterized by the presence of these malignant tumor cells.
